title: Helodium inundatum
intro: Helodium inundatum, also known as Marsh Shield Fern, is a rare and critically endangered species of fern native to North America. It is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may aid in reducing inflammation and protecting against cell damage. The bioactive constituents of Helodium inundatum include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins, which are believed to contribute to its medicinal properties. Traditional herbal preparations made from Marsh Shield Fern include teas and infusions, although the plant's rarity and endangered status limit its availability for commercial use.
taxonomy: Helodium inundatum is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Cyperaceae. It belongs to the genus Helodium, which consists of aquatic plants found in freshwater environments. Helodium inundatum is a type of sedge that grows in dense stands, often in shallow water. This plant is typically found in temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere, where it thrives in areas with standing water and full sun to partial shade. The classification of Helodium inundatum reflects its unique characteristics and adaptations as an aquatic plant species.
